error: When it’s done, two branches will lie by the squirrel.
erroneous_event:

Bind.
story:
Freezing bark scrapes my face as I cling to an old oak. Winter has stripped the forest bare, but the oak still holds its magic, refusing to sleep. Hunger claws my belly—two days without food—and I climb. The wind wants me gone. “Steady as an oak” never chased a squirrel.

Four branches up, two over: a hollow where I saw it hide. I time my moves between gusts, reach around a bulge in the trunk, lean too far, and the branch is ripped out from under me. I slam into the trunk, twigs rake my skin, and I scrabble until my foot finds a knot. I haul myself higher, press close, and find the squirrel. Magic cracks along the scar in my right palm. I shape it, touch fur, and whisper an intention.

Break.

The squirrel goes limp—clean, quick. The branch gives way. I fall.

Branches whip past. The ground slams my right arm with a crunch that empties my lungs. Cold crawls from elbow to neck. Not enough breath to scream, which is a blessing. I sit up in jerks of pain and look: broken. Again.

Mother used to say young bones heal fast. I press against the oak and make myself still. Slow the heart. Slower. The ground seeps cold. The bark bites my spine. I reach for both and ask for more.

Heat floods the soil under me. The tree resists. Living things are harder to draw from, the nearer they cling to life. I push, and a frost-thin barrier breaks. For a heartbeat I see what the tree sees: roots tangled deep, stubborn leaves in a gale, branches filtering the sky. I am part of it and apart.

Balance matters. I choose a branch high above, wrap my will along its length. The scar in my left palm prickles hot. I set it to my shattered elbow. When it’s done, two branches will lie by the squirrel.

Bind.

A branch snaps overhead as my bones slam together. Now I scream.

When I can see, the dirt is ash around my legs, grass shriveled to dust. Two branches lie near the squirrel: one burned down to splinters, one furred with lichen and fresh shoots, mushrooms blossoming along its length. Balance for my breaking.

I flex my arm—good enough—and grin. Mother warned me off squirrels. Not worth the danger. Mother was wrong about a lot.

A growl rakes the air behind me. It’s found me.

I flatten to the trunk and tuck the squirrel into my belt. The creature is tall and climbs fast; a tree is a trap. I run. Its howl scrapes the forest clean of birds. Breath burns. Stink of musk and decay chases me. I feint at the last instant, dive into a briar hollow, crawl through torn skin and falling debris, burst out, sprint for the cave.

Home is a seam in stone hidden by ivy. Inside, I touch the laid twigs and say, Burn. Heat blooms along the scar across my lower back. I hate that one most. A tiny purple-flowered vine noses up through the dirt by my foot. Balance again.

Claws scuff stone. The monster fills the entrance, patchy fur and six-inch talons, spine ridged, breath fogging. “You know the rules,” I say. Its growl smothers the fire until I add, “Last one home has to share.” It lowers its head, drags in a hare.

I skin and split the meat, toss the hides. He swallows them whole and sulks by the door. I coax the fire back and go to him. He turns away, then leans into my hand when I scratch his neck.

He should have eaten me when my mother died. That’s how the forest works. Instead, we made a different rule: share or die. He is old magic—soil, rot, necessary as rain—not the corruption Mother named. I still fear him; fear keeps me alive. But there’s respect threaded through what binds us.

Two monsters in the forest, surviving each other.
